did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and machine learning to detect skin lesions. Specifically, it employs a hybrid model that fuses handcrafted features (color and texture via GLCM) and patient metadata with transfer-learned image features from EfficientNet B4-B7 backbones. These inputs are processed through a multi-input single-output (MISO) artificial neural network classifier, with predictions ensembled using majority soft voting. The study was conducted on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 dermatology datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid model that fuses handcrafted features, patient metadata, and deep learning (EfficientNets) for skin lesion detection. It details specific methodology, datasets (ISIC2018, ISIC2019), training procedures, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., 90.49% sensitivity on ISIC2018), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detecting skin lesions fusing handcrafted features in image network ensembles
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning and machine learning model to automate the detection and classification of skin lesions, specifically targeting melanoma and other cancerous classes. The approach fuses transfer-learned features from EfficientNet B4-B7 backbones, handcrafted color and texture features extracted via GLCM, and patient-centric metadata within a multi-input single-output artificial neural network. The system was trained and evaluated on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets, utilizing ensemble strategies such as majority soft voting and weighted voting to address class imbalance. The model achieved a sensitivity of 90.49% and specificity of 97.76% on ISIC2018, and 85.58% sensitivity with 98.29% specificity on ISIC2019. These results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid ensemble method outperforms several state-of-the-art models in skin lesion classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Cancerous category in ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets; identified as the deadliest skin cancer and a primary focus of the study.
Cancerous category in ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets; noted for high visual similarity to BKL and SCC.
Additional cancerous category present in ISIC2019 dataset but not in ISIC2018.
Benign category; most frequent class in both datasets (approx. 66% in ISIC2018, 50% in ISIC2019).
Benign category present in both ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.
Benign category present in both ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.
Benign category present in both ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets; noted as having least representation.
Benign category present in both ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets; noted as having least representation.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'classify skin lesion dermoscopic images' and 'categorize lesions'. The model outputs a 'probability distribution vector for all classes' using a softmax activation function. The study addresses multi-class classification across 7 classes in ISIC2018 and 8 classes in ISIC2019, evaluating performance via sensitivity, specificity, and balanced accuracy for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenge containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions with associated patient-specific metadata. It contains 7 skin lesion classes (5 benign, 2 cancerous).

use.training: Used for training the hybrid model (EfficientNet backbones, handcrafted features, and metadata) with an 8:1:1 split for training, validation, and testing.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training with an 8:1:1 split.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model, achieving 90.49% sensitivity and 97.76% specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A larger and more divergent public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenge containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions with associated patient-specific metadata. It contains 8 skin lesion classes (6 benign, 2 cancerous).

use.training: Used for training the hybrid model (EfficientNet backbones, handcrafted features, and metadata) with an 8:1:1 split for training, validation, and testing.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training with an 8:1:1 split.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model, achieving 85.58% sensitivity and 98.29% specificity.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Ensemble strategy to combine predictions from multiple models to reduce variance and improve classification accuracy.
Ensemble strategy to combine predictions by assigning weights to models based on their contribution to accuracy or sensitivity.
To estimate optimal model weights for the weighted ensemble by searching for weight combinations that maximize accuracy and sensitivity.
Evaluation metric to measure the average true positive rate across all classes, addressing class imbalance.
Evaluation metric to measure the average true negative rate across all classes.
Evaluation metric computed by averaging true positive and true negative rates to assess performance on imbalanced datasets.
Evaluation metric derived from ROC curves to assess model discrimination capability.
Used to plot actual vs. predicted lesion classes to calculate True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives for each class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, and AUC, and compared qualitatively to state-of-the-art models.

ISIC2018.metric: Mean Sensitivity, Mean Specificity, Balanced Accuracy
ISIC2018.notes: The most sensitive system was the ensemble model weighted based on maximum sensitivity with a mean sensitivity of 90.50% and balanced accuracy of 94.13%. The model recognized exactly 47 out of 50 BCC cases and 94 out of 112 Melanoma cases. AUC was over 98.00%.

ISIC2019.metric: Mean Sensitivity, Mean Specificity, Balanced Accuracy
ISIC2019.notes: The ensemble strategy based on maximum sensitivity outperformed other models. The model exhibited an overall accuracy of 91.93%. AUC was over 98.00%. Performance on the new class SCC was limited due to high visual similarity with BCC and BKL.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Multi-Input Single-Output (MISO) model fusing EfficientNet backbones, handcrafted features, and patient metadata, ensembled via majority soft voting and weighted majority voting.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from pre-trained EfficientNet models (B4, B5, B6, B7) published by Google in 2019. Initial transfer learning for 10 epochs with frozen layers, followed by fine-tuning for 30 epochs with unfrozen layers.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels for images; 8 distinct handcrafted features (color variegation and GLCM statistics); preprocessed patient metadata.

architecture_details.backbone: EfficientNet variants (B4, B5, B6, B7) with compound scaling technique.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Handcrafted features extracted via UNet segmentation (for hair removal/ROI) and Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) at four angles (0, π/2, π, 3π/2) with pixel spacing 1, yielding contrast, energy, homogeneity, dissimilarity, and correlation. Color variegation calculated as standard deviation of intensity distribution across RGB channels normalized by max intensity.
architecture_details.metadata_branch: Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) with two dense layers (256 neurons in second layer), Batch Normalization, ReLU activation, and 25% Dropout after first dense layer.
architecture_details.fusion: Concatenation of flattened CNN features (1280 features) and MLP metadata features (256 features) into a 1536-dimensional vector.
architecture_details.classification_branch: Multi-input fully connected network: Initial dense layer (1024 latent representations) with Batch Normalization, ReLU, and 40% Dropout; final dense layer with Softmax activation for probability distribution.
architecture_details.ensemble: Majority soft voting and weighted majority voting (weights optimized via grid search for max accuracy and max sensitivity) combining predictions from EfficientNet B4-B7 models with and without handcrafted features.

training_details.loss_function: Categorical Cross Entropy for classification; Binary Cross-Entropy for UNet segmentation.
training_details.epochs: 40 total (10 for transfer learning, 30 for fine-tuning).
training_details.learning_rate: 1e-3 for initial 10 epochs; 1e-4 for fine-tuning (reduced by half if validation loss did not improve for 3 consecutive epochs).
training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotation, translation, and flipping by a factor of 0.1.
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Class weights assigned based on dataset distribution (e.g., DF weight 12.437 in ISIC2018; NV weight 0.213 in ISIC2018).

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability distribution vector for all skin lesion classes (7 classes for ISIC2018, 8 classes for ISIC2019).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multi-Input Single-Output (MISO) model with image data and metadata
Multi-Input Single-Output (MISO) model with image data, handcrafted features, and metadata
preprocessing_techniques: Dull razor hair removal algorithm (black hat transformation, morphological closing with 5x5 structuring element, binary thresholding, and inpainting with radius 3) to remove human hair strands and noise.
UNet segmentation architecture for lesion segmentation to generate binary masks.
Feature selection on metadata (removal of lesion ID and diagnosis type).
Missing value handling in metadata using mean substitution for numerical attributes and maximum frequency imputation for categorical attributes.
One-hot encoding for categorical metadata values.
Min-Max normalization of combined features (handcrafted features and metadata) to scale values to [0, 1].
Data augmentation via random rotation, translation, and flipping by a factor of 0.1.
Class weight assignment to handle class imbalance.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images with a factor of 0.1 each to regularize the model and avoid over-fitting.
Introduced during training to balance data, assigning higher weights to minority classes (e.g., DF weight 12.437 in ISIC2018; NV weight 0.213 in ISIC2018).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid ensemble model, which fuses transfer-learned features from EfficientNet B4-B7 backbones, handcrafted color and texture features (via GLCM), and patient metadata, effectively automates skin lesion classification.

The weighted ensemble strategy based on maximum sensitivity outperformed individual models and majority voting, achieving a mean sensitivity of 90.50% and balanced accuracy of 94.13% on the ISIC2018 dataset.
On the larger and more divergent ISIC2019 dataset, the model achieved a mean sensitivity of 85.58% and mean specificity of 98.29%, with an overall accuracy of 91.93%.
The model demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art models, outperforming them by a margin of at least 6% on ISIC2018.
The model achieved an Area under the Curve (AUC) of over 98.00% for both datasets.
The model showed high sensitivity for cancerous classes, correctly identifying 47 out of 50 Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) cases and 94 out of 112 Melanoma cases in the ISIC2018 test set.
The model's performance was slightly lower on the ISIC2019 dataset compared to ISIC2018, likely due to the heterogeneous nature of ISIC2019 compared to the relatively homogeneous ISIC2018.
Classification of Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) in ISIC2019 was limited due to high visual similarity with Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Benign Keratosis (BKL).
A limitation of the model is the tendency to misclassify benign Nevi as Melanoma, although this is considered acceptable for an initial diagnostic aid.
The inclusion of patient-centric metadata and handcrafted features provided a significant advantage in classification performance over models using only image data.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on public datasets (ISIC2018 and ISIC2019) without external clinical validation, limiting generalizability to diverse real-world populations.
The model exhibits a significant bias towards classifying benign Melanocytic Nevi as Melanoma, which is noted as a limitation but deemed acceptable for an initial diagnostic aid.
Performance on the ISIC2019 dataset was lower than on ISIC2018, attributed to the heterogeneous nature of ISIC2019 compared to the relatively homogeneous ISIC2018.
Classification of Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) was limited due to high visual similarity with Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Benign Keratosis (BKL).
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are qualitative or based on descriptive metrics.
The model's sensitivity for Melanoma (84-85%) is reported as higher than the clinical recognition rate (70%), which may indicate optimistic bias or dataset-specific characteristics.
The study addresses class imbalance using class weights and specific ensemble strategies, but the extreme imbalance (e.g., NV comprising ~66% of ISIC2018) remains a fundamental dataset limitation.

The authors claim the model outperforms state-of-the-art models by at least 6%, but this comparison is limited to specific benchmarks cited in the paper and may not represent the full landscape of current methods.
